## Home (EN)  <https://lexsas.com/>
============================================================
AI LEGAL TECH
Legal workflow automation, with the guardrails built in
LEXSAS is the AI legal-tech venture I founded in 2026. I design and advise on tools that take the manual work out of legal and regulatory operations, from intake and document workflows to research and compliance, with privacy, IP and governance guardrails in place from the start.
Get in touch
Explore focus areas
20+ years in-house
Amazon, eBay, PMI, White & Case
Statutory Board Director
Ankara Bar
FOCUS AREAS
Six areas, one operating standard
From intake automation to competition defense, every engagement ships with its guardrails.
Legal tech and AI workflow automation
Designing and advising on AI-enabled tooling for legal work: intake, document and knowledge workflows, research and compliance operations. The aim is fewer manual steps without a lower standard of review.
AI governance and regulatory compliance
Privacy, IP and governance guardrails that ship with the tool rather than after it, alongside model oversight, human review and documentation. I work on the line between what a policy says and what a team can actually operate.
GenAI transformation for legal teams
Legal operations strategy for generative AI: where to start, what to build versus buy, and how responsible adoption actually happens. I have co-developed GenAI legal tooling in-house on AWS Bedrock and run the pilots that followed.
Data protection and data sovereignty
Cross-border data transfer and localisation questions under KVKK and GDPR, including how to keep transfers lawful when a regulator changes its position. I have executed a first-in-market data transfer undertaking under KVKK.
E-commerce and platform regulation
Marketplace and platform rules across consumer protection, advertising, intermediary liability and payments touchpoints. This includes licensing perimeter questions and the controls that keep a platform compliant at scale.
Competition and digital markets
Antitrust strategy for platforms, covering parity clauses, ranking practices and algorithmic accountability. I have led a competition defense on pricing algorithms through to file closure with no penalty.
APPROACH
How I work
Practitioner, not vendor
I have built and run these tools inside a legal team, not sold them. Advice starts from what a workflow actually looks like, not from a product roadmap.
Guardrails that enable speed
The useful answer is rarely a flat yes or no; it is yes, if. Well-designed controls let a team move quickly while protecting the business over the long term.
Measurable pilots, human review
Every AI deployment should begin as a pilot with a defined measure of success and a person owning the output. If it cannot show what it changed, it has not earned a wider rollout.
“The useful answer is rarely a flat yes or no; it is yes, if.”
The operating principle
EXPERIENCE
Twenty years across the table from regulators
I have spent 20+ years in-house with global technology and marketplace businesses, blending legal directorship with regulatory affairs, corporate affairs and public policy leadership, most of it in regulated markets.
That includes statutory board roles and leading legal functions through investigations, licensing questions and market entry. What I say about AI stands on this practitioner ground.
Legal and regulatory leadership, in-house
Statutory board directorship
Ankara Bar

FAQ
Common questions
What does LEXSAS do?
LEXSAS is an AI legal-tech venture focused on legal workflow automation and advisory. It designs AI-enabled tooling for legal and regulatory teams, from intake and document workflows to research and compliance operations, and advises on responsible adoption with privacy, IP and governance guardrails built in.
Which areas does the advisory work cover?
Legal technology and AI governance, regulatory affairs and corporate affairs in regulated digital markets, e-commerce and platform regulation, data protection under KVKK and GDPR, and competition questions in digital markets. Behind it sit 20+ years of in-house work as legal director and regulatory affairs leader with global technology, consumer and marketplace businesses.
Who is behind LEXSAS?
Asim Serdar Yilmaz, founder of LEXSAS and a senior legal and regulatory affairs leader with more than two decades in-house at Amazon, eBay and Philip Morris International, following private practice at White & Case.
Do you work in English and Turkish?
Yes. Advisory, writing and speaking are available in both languages, from Istanbul and remotely.
Is this legal representation?
No. LEXSAS provides informational consultancy on legal technology, AI governance and regulatory strategy. It is not a law firm and does not provide legal representation.
CONTACT
Let’s talk.
serdar@lexsas.com
Advisory on AI governance, regulated digital markets and legal operations. Speaking and board conversations are welcome, in English or Turkish.

## About (EN)  <https://lexsas.com/about.html>
============================================================
Page title: Asim Serdar Yilmaz | Legal and Regulatory Affairs Leader · LEXSAS
ABOUT
Asim Serdar Yilmaz: two decades inside regulated technology businesses
I am Asim Serdar Yilmaz, a senior in-house legal leader based in Istanbul. Over more than 20 years I have advised global technology, consumer and regulated businesses across Turkey and the Middle East, in retail and marketplace, streaming and media, advertising, payments, data and IP. My work tends to sit where a business wants to move quickly and a regulator wants to move carefully.
I started in private practice at White & Case in Ankara, on cross-border M&A, privatisations and IP in regulated sectors. From there I moved in-house: first into a strict, high-scrutiny compliance environment at a global group in a highly regulated industry across the Middle East, then into e-commerce at eBay's Turkey and MENA business, and from 2019 to 2026 as Legal Director for Amazon Turkey. In 2026 I moved from legal into regulatory affairs, and today I lead the regulatory agenda within the corporate affairs function of a global group in a highly regulated industry.
The thread through all of it is regulated markets. I have spent years turning regulatory ambiguity into paths a business can actually take, and building what I think of as "yes, if" guardrails: controls that let a team move at speed while protecting the enterprise over the long term. Much of that has meant sitting across the table from competition authorities, data protection regulators, media regulators and ministries.
The other current chapter is LEXSAS, the AI legal-tech venture I founded in 2026, focused on legal workflow automation and advisory. It blends what the career built in layers: legal directorship, regulatory affairs, corporate affairs and public policy, now paired with hands-on AI governance. It grew out of years of building generative AI tooling for legal work, on AWS Bedrock among other platforms, and of seeing where adoption succeeds and where it quietly fails. This site is where I write about that intersection of law, regulation and AI, as informational consultancy rather than legal representation.
Career
2026-present
Founder
LEXSAS, Istanbul
An AI legal-tech venture focused on legal workflow automation and advisory for legal and regulatory teams.
Designs and advises on AI-enabled tooling across the legal workflow: intake, document and knowledge management, research and compliance operations.
Builds privacy, IP and governance guardrails into every tool from the start, pairing 20+ years of legal and regulatory leadership, most of it in-house, with responsible AI adoption.
2026-present
Regulatory Affairs Director
A global group in a highly regulated consumer industry, Istanbul
Leads the regulatory agenda within the corporate affairs function, as functional lead in one of the most heavily regulated consumer sectors.
Regulatory strategy, horizon scanning and regulatory-change analysis for the Turkish market, supporting public policy and government relations work.
Applies AI governance discipline to regulatory operations: monitoring, analysis and institutional knowledge workflows, with guardrails built in.
2019-2026
Legal Director, Technology, E-Commerce and Media; Board Director
Amazon Turkey, Istanbul
End-to-end retail and marketplace counsel: commercial contracting, consumer law, advertising, product compliance, supply chain, payments, privacy and disputes.
Regulatory representation before RTUK, the Turkish Competition Authority, the Ministry of Trade, the Ministry of Industry and Technology, the KVKK Authority and the Central Bank, including representation in a Ministry of Trade investigation.
Led the response to a KVKK order on cross-border data transfers: prepared and executed a Data Transfer Undertaking approved by the KVKK Authority.
Competition and digital markets strategy on parity and MFN, ranking and Buy Box practices, and algorithmic accountability, including leading the Turkey defense strategy on a pricing-algorithms investigation before the Turkish Competition Authority.

eBay Turkey (GittiGidiyor), Istanbul
Regional legal leadership for e-commerce marketplace operations across Turkey and MENA: consumer protection, advertising, payments, IP and commercial transactions.
Head of Public Policy, representing the company before regulators and industry bodies, including the TOBB E-Commerce Assembly.
Payments and fintech: designed and secured regulator approval for a commercial agent exemption model enabling marketplace payment collection and seller payouts without a full payment institution license.
Regional Business Ethics Officer under the FCPA and UK Bribery Act: Code of Conduct training, third-party due diligence and internal investigations.
2008-2012

A global group in a highly regulated industry, Dubai and Lausanne
Advised commercial teams across 12 Middle East markets under strict advertising bans and Ministry of Health regulations.
Route-to-market and distribution architecture: distribution, key account and trade marketing arrangements, with templates and controls.
Company Secretary for regional entities, coordinating external counsel across 12 jurisdictions.

Between 2019 and 2026 I served as a Statutory Board Director and authorised representative for multiple Amazon entities in Turkey, including Prime Video Turkey and Project Kuiper, under Turkish company law. Earlier I was a Deputy Board Member of the e-Commerce Commercial Enterprises Association. These roles carried director duties and liability under the Turkish Commercial Code.

## Insights (EN)  <https://lexsas.com/insights.html>
============================================================
INSIGHTS
Notes on law, regulation and AI
Short, practitioner pieces on the questions I keep returning to: how AI governance works in practice, how regulated digital markets are changing, and where legal operations can genuinely improve.
July 2026 - AI regulation
The EU AI Act on 2 August 2026: what actually applies
The high-risk deadlines moved to 2027 and 2028. The Article 50 transparency duties did not: chatbots, synthetic content and deepfakes are in scope from 2 August 2026.
July 2026 - Legal AI
AI citation sanctions keep climbing: a verification protocol for legal teams
More than 1,700 court decisions involve AI-hallucinated filings. A five-step verification protocol before anything leaves the team.
July 2026 - E-commerce law
Marketplace intermediary liability in Turkey: three regimes to read together
A marketplace's liability in Turkey sits where three regimes meet: e-commerce Law 6563, consumer Law 6502, and the 5651 content regime.
July 2026 - Platform regulation
Marketplace ranking and algorithmic accountability: questions for the legal lead
Ranking is now a legal surface under the DSA, DMA, P2B rules and Turkey's e-commerce law. What an in-house legal lead should ask about it.
July 2026 - Legal AI
Legal AI: build, buy, or configure?
Most legal teams should configure platforms, buy for commodity tasks, and build only the most sensitive work. A practitioner's decision frame.
July 2026 - Legal operations
How to measure a legal automation pilot
A pilot without metrics is a demo. Baseline the workflow, set error tolerances per task, and fix a kill-or-scale threshold before you start.
July 2026 - AI governance
AI governance for in-house legal teams: five minimum controls
Five minimum controls an in-house legal team needs before scaling generative AI: data handling, accountability, human review, records, error handling.
July 2026 - Data protection
KVKK-compliant generative AI workflows for legal teams
What KVKK expects when legal teams route personal data through generative AI: lawful basis, cross-border transfer mechanisms, minimization, records.
July 2026 - Legal operations
An AI vendor due diligence checklist for legal teams
Six questions a GC should ask an AI vendor before signing: training data, prompt retention, subprocessors, IP indemnity, logging, and exit terms.
July 2026 - Legal market
The billable hour is running out of logic
AI deflates the hour itself. Next generation firms price the work, not the time.
July 2026 - Legal AI
AI is quietly redrawing the legal industry
Adoption doubled in a year. The advantage is shifting from headcount to judgment and workflow design.
July 2026 - Legal operations
Where a lawyer's hours actually go
The average lawyer bills a fraction of the day. What workflow automation recovers, and how to start.
July 2026 - AI governance
What generative AI actually changes for in-house legal teams
Where generative AI earns its place in legal work, and where careful human judgment still has to sit.

## LinkedIn (EN) (personal LinkedIn post by the founder; opinion commentary, quoted verbatim)  <https://lexsas.com/linkedin.html>
============================================================
LINKEDIN
Notes from my LinkedIn feed
I post short observations on AI policy, legal tech and regulated digital markets. A selection is below; the rest live on LinkedIn.
Asim Serdar Yilmaz
July 2026
Most "AI governance" debates skip the part legal and regulatory teams actually need: the guardrails.
After 20+ years as a lawyer across tech, e-commerce and heavily regulated markets, and now building AI tools for legal work myself, here is the practical guardrail set I come back to before any team "adopts AI":
1) Privacy. Where does the data go, what is the lawful basis, and can you still honour KVKK/GDPR rights (access, deletion, cross-border transfer) once a model sits on top? If you cannot answer that in one paragraph, you are not ready.
2) IP and confidentiality. Who owns the output? What happens to the input: is it retained or used for training? Is privileged or confidential material leaving your perimeter?
3) Transparency and accountability. Can a human explain why the system produced an answer, and is that disclosed where disclosure is required (consumer-facing, regulator-facing)? AI drafts; a person owns the judgment.
4) Vendor risk. Model changes, sub-processors, data-retention limits and audit rights belong in the contract, not the FAQ.
The pattern I have seen work: do not ban AI, and do not rubber-stamp it. Build a "yes, if" layer: clear conditions that let teams move fast while protecting the enterprise. Governance that only says "no" gets routed around. Governance that enables adoption gets used.
What would you add to the list?
#AIGovernance - #RegulatoryAffairs - #LegalOps - #Privacy - #PublicPolicy
Asim Serdar Yilmaz
July 2026
AI will not replace regulatory teams. But regulatory teams that use AI will outpace the ones that do not.
The unglamorous, high-leverage use cases I am most interested in:
• Horizon scanning: monitoring legislative and regulatory change across markets and surfacing what actually matters, faster.
• Analysis and synthesis: turning a 90-page draft regulation into a clean, business-ready brief.
• Knowledge management: making years of institutional regulatory memory searchable instead of trapped in inboxes.
The catch: every one of these needs governance baked in: privacy, IP, transparency, and a human accountable for the final call.
The line I hold: use AI to remove the grunt work, never to outsource the judgment.
#AIGovernance - #RegulatoryStrategy - #LegalTech - #AI
Asim Serdar Yilmaz
July 2026
I started Lexsas because I kept hitting the same wall in-house: the legal and regulatory work that is easiest to automate is also the work most exposed to privacy, IP and confidentiality risk. So the interesting problem is not "can AI do this?" It is "can AI do this safely enough that a regulated business will actually use it?"
That question, responsible AI adoption for legal and regulatory teams, is what I spend my spare cycles on now.
Asim Serdar Yilmaz
July 2026
On June 12, the AI sitting inside a lot of everyday workflows just stopped working.
On June 26, OpenAI previewed GPT-5.6 Sol to a small group of trusted partners. OpenAI stated: "We don't believe this kind of government access process should become the long-term default."
On June 12, 2026, Anthropic removed access to its frontier AI models Fable 5 and Mythos 5. Its statement: "We are complying with the government's legal directive and are removing access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all users." Export controls were lifted on July 1.
Epictetus sorted things into what we control and what we don't. I quote that line more often than I live by it. June was the opening scene where the camera lingers on a pistol for a moment, then moves on. We all know that pistol goes off before the credits.
My read: AI bans are joining a sanctions toolkit the world already knows. A year or two from now, a cutoff would not play out like frozen offshore accounts. It could bring ordinary life close to a standstill. An explicit AI policy and open-source fallbacks are the hedge. The next episode will show who prepared.
#AIPolicy - #DigitalSovereignty - #AIGeopolitics - #TechDiplomacy
View the post on LinkedIn
Follow along
New posts appear on LinkedIn first. Follow me there for short, practitioner takes between the longer pieces on this site.

## Article: The EU AI Act on 2 August 2026: what actually applies  <https://lexsas.com/insights/eu-ai-act-august-2026.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 3 min read
The EU AI Act on 2 August 2026: what actually applies
For two years, 2 August 2026 was the date every AI compliance plan revolved around. In June the calendar was redrawn: the Digital Omnibus, adopted by the European Parliament on 16 June and by the Council on 29 June, moves the high-risk obligations for Annex III systems to 2 December 2027 and the rules for AI embedded in regulated products to 2 August 2028. The act now awaits publication in the Official Journal.
The common misreading is to treat that as a pause. One block of the regulation was not postponed: the Article 50 transparency obligations, which apply from 2 August 2026 to any organisation whose AI interacts with people or publishes content.
In practice, three duties matter for most companies. A chatbot must disclose that the user is talking to AI at the start of the interaction. AI-generated text, image, audio and video must carry machine-readable marking. Deepfakes, and AI-generated text published to inform the public, must be visibly labelled. The Commission's Code of Practice on marking and labelling, published on 10 June 2026, is the ready-made path to show conformity.
Enforcement arrives on the same date. From 2 August 2026, national market surveillance authorities can fine breaches of these duties up to 15 million euro or 3 percent of worldwide annual turnover. Two narrow transitions exist: providers of generative systems already on the market have until 2 December 2026 to make machine-readable marking work, and a new ban on AI systems that generate non-consensual intimate imagery applies from that December date.
The rest of the timeline was already live. The prohibited-practice bans and the AI literacy duty have applied since February 2025, and the obligations for general-purpose AI models since August 2025, with the voluntary GPAI Code of Practice as the mainstream compliance route for providers.
For a legal team, three moves before the date. First, inventory every AI surface that talks to people or publishes outward, including marketing, customer service and product features. Second, check that disclosures are switched on and visible at first contact, not buried in terms of use. Third, write down for each surface whether you sit in the provider or deployer seat: both carry duties, and white-labelling a vendor's tool can move you into the provider one. The high-risk work bought time. Transparency did not.
Sources
European Commission, AI Act policy page (application timeline), July 2026
Council of the EU, final green light to the Digital Omnibus on AI, 29 June 2026
European Commission, Code of Practice on Transparency of AI-Generated Content, June 2026
Related: Legal tech and AI workflow automation

============================================================
## Article: AI citation sanctions keep climbing: a verification protocol for legal teams  <https://lexsas.com/insights/ai-citation-verification.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 3 min read
AI citation sanctions keep climbing: a verification protocol for legal teams
The counter only moves one way. Damien Charlotin's public database of court decisions engaging with AI-hallucinated filings passed 1,700 entries this month, the majority from US courts, with several new decisions added every working day.
The escalation is easy to trace. In 2023, Mata v. Avianca cost two lawyers $5,000 and made global news. In 2026 alone, the Sixth Circuit ordered two attorneys to pay $15,000 each on top of the opposing side's fees, an Oregon winery dispute produced roughly $110,000 in aggregate sanctions and a dismissal, and Nebraska suspended a lawyer from practice until further order of the court, pending full disciplinary proceedings, over AI-hallucinated citations. On 3 June, the Ninth Circuit suspended two lawyers from its bar for six months, fined them $2,500 each, and ordered them to disclose any AI use in their filings for the next two years.
Read the orders and one pattern holds. Courts do not sanction the use of AI; they sanction signing and filing what was not checked. The Ninth Circuit put it plainly: the rules are not violated at the point of research and drafting, but at the point of signing and filing. Severity then tracks the response. Lawyers who disclosed the error promptly and corrected it received warnings. Lawyers who blamed typos, juniors or another AI tool received suspensions. Hundreds of US judges also run their own standing orders on AI in filings, tracked by Ropes and Gray, so the local rule needs checking before every submission.
My verification protocol has five steps, none optional for anything that leaves the team. One, open every citation in the official reporter or database; never ask a second AI whether the first AI's case exists. Two, compare every quotation against the opinion word by word. Three, test the proposition: the case must actually say what the draft claims it says. Four, test validity: the authority must still be good law. Five, a named person signs the verification note before the document goes out. The same five steps apply to client memos and advice, not only to court filings.
In-house teams have one extra lever: outside counsel. Put in the engagement letter how the firm verifies AI-assisted citations and who certifies it per filing. Turkish bar guidance now lands in the same place: the Union of Turkish Bar Associations' advisory guide for lawyers, published in June 2026, puts the duty to verify AI output and the final responsibility squarely on the lawyer. The tool is welcome in the workflow. The judgment never leaves it.
Sources
Damien Charlotin, AI Hallucination Cases database, July 2026
ABA Journal, Attorneys' lack of candor over AI errors leads to stricter sanctions, 4 June 2026
Ropes and Gray, Artificial Intelligence Court Order Tracker
Union of Turkish Bar Associations, AI and lawyering workshop outcome panel, 20 June 2026
Related: Legal tech and AI workflow automation

============================================================
## Article: Marketplace intermediary liability in Turkey: three regimes to read together  <https://lexsas.com/insights/turkey-marketplace-liability.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
Marketplace intermediary liability in Turkey: three regimes to read together
I spent years running legal and regulatory work for online marketplaces in Turkey, and the question that came up most was deceptively simple. When a third party sells something on our platform, what are we on the hook for? The honest answer is that no single statute holds it. A marketplace's exposure sits where three regimes overlap, and the work is to read them as one map rather than three separate texts.
The first is the E-Commerce Law, No. 6563, reshaped by the 2022 amendments that introduced a tiered set of obligations for large intermediary service providers. The second is consumer protection under Law No. 6502, which sets duties around pre-contract information, the defective goods process, and refunds when a transaction goes wrong. The third is the internet content regime under Law No. 5651. Its main personal-rights takedown article was annulled by the Constitutional Court in October 2024 and is now awaiting re-legislation, which is exactly why a platform cannot read it in isolation and needs the combined map.
The volume behind these questions is not small. The Ministry of Trade reported total e-commerce volume in Turkey at 3 trillion lira in 2024, up 61.7 percent on the year (ticaret.gov.tr). More sellers and more listings mean more moments where the three regimes meet a single transaction.
The practical work is mapping them together. A single listing or complaint can raise duties under more than one regime at once: a takedown question, a consumer remedy, and a record-keeping obligation, side by side. Reading one regime in isolation is how a compliant-looking process still leaves a gap. The artifact that closes it is a current, per-regime duty map, each duty tied to an owner and a legal basis.
Sources
Ticaret Bakanlığı, Türkiye'de E-Ticaretin Görünümü Raporu (2025)
Related: E-commerce and platform regulation

============================================================
## Article: Marketplace ranking and algorithmic accountability: questions for the legal lead  <https://lexsas.com/insights/marketplace-algorithmic-accountability.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
Marketplace ranking and algorithmic accountability: questions for the legal lead
I spent several years on the platform side, close to the teams that build and tune ranking systems. Back then ranking was a product question. It is now a legal one, and the legal lead is usually the last person to be told how the system works.
Three regimes have turned the search box into a documented surface. The EU Digital Services Act requires large marketplaces to disclose the main parameters of their recommender systems, a transparency duty that scales by size and bites hardest on the very large platforms. The Platform-to-Business Regulation applies to online intermediation services and requires the main ranking parameters to be set out in the terms and conditions. Turkey's amended Electronic Commerce Law adds its own ranking and advertising transparency duties on intermediary service providers.
The enforcement is real. On 23 April 2025 the European Commission issued its first non-compliance decisions under the Digital Markets Act, fining Apple 500 million euros over anti-steering restrictions and Meta 200 million euros over its consent-or-pay model. Neither decision was about ranking, but the DMA separately prohibits self-preferencing in ranking as a breach in its own right.
So my questions to the product team are concrete. Which parameters move a listing up or down, and can we state them plainly? Where do our own products sit in that logic? Who can adjust the weighting, and is that change logged? The law asks for the main parameters; good governance keeps a decision log too. If a business user or a regulator asks why a competitor ranks above them, can we answer from records rather than from memory?
A ranking system that cannot explain itself is a liability waiting for a complaint. The time to write the explanation down is before someone demands it.
Sources
European Commission, first DMA non-compliance decisions, 23 April 2025
Hunton Andrews Kurth, summary of the Apple and Meta DMA fines (2025)
Related: E-commerce and platform regulation

============================================================
## Article: Legal AI: build, buy, or configure?  <https://lexsas.com/insights/legal-ai-build-buy-configure.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
Legal AI: build, buy, or configure?
I have built legal AI tooling on AWS Bedrock inside a large platform company, and bought vendor tools too. The real choice is rarely build or buy. You can buy a finished product, adapt an enterprise platform to your workflow, or build your own. That middle path is configure, and most work belongs there.
Three questions decide it: how sensitive the data is, how specific the workflow is, and how much engineering the team can carry.
Configure is the default. You still buy the platform; what you own is the workflow on top. Enterprise platforms let you wire custom workflows, retrieval, and guardrails onto a hosted model you do not own. For contract review and internal knowledge, this captures most of the value for far less maintenance.
Buy for commodity tasks. Transcription, translation, and first-pass summarization are mature, well-served categories. Building them in-house means a year rebuilding what you could license this week.
Build only for workflows that are both sensitive and differentiating. Building does not remove third parties: our own Bedrock tooling still runs on cloud infrastructure. It is the right call when off-the-shelf terms cannot meet your requirements on data use, retention, residency, and audit, and the workflow itself sets you apart. You still carry the full lifecycle burden.
Bloomberg Law reported in 2026 that for roughly 67 percent of large law firms, new-technology spending stayed under 2 percent of the 2025 budget. It counts firms, not in-house teams, but the signal carries.
Capacity decides the rest. ILTA's 2024 survey found generative AI use ranging from 20 percent at small firms to 74 percent at the largest. My read: that gap follows engineering capacity more than ambition.
Match the tool to the workflow: configure first, buy the commodity, build only what you cannot safely hand to anyone else.
Sources
ILTA 2024 Technology Survey (via eDiscovery Today)
Bloomberg Law, law firm tech budgets and the build-versus-buy AI debate (2026)
Related: Legal tech and AI workflow automation

============================================================
## Article: How to measure a legal automation pilot  <https://lexsas.com/insights/measuring-automation-pilots.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
How to measure a legal automation pilot
A pilot without metrics is a demo. It looks impressive in the room, everyone nods, and three months later no one can say whether it worked. I have run enough of these in a large in-house team to know the plan matters more than the tool.
Start by baselining the workflow before the model touches it. Time the cycle end to end. Count the touches: how many people handle a task and how often it changes hands. Total external spend: outside counsel hours and per-matter fees. Treat these three as the minimum baseline, with quality metrics, accuracy, rework, and error severity, beside them.
Then define the error types each task produces. I sort them into three: critical errors that change a legal outcome, material errors that need real rework, and formal errors that are cosmetic. Set the tolerance per class, zero for the critical one, and write it down before the pilot.
Measure reviewer time, not model time. The model answers in seconds; what matters is how long a lawyer checks and fixes it. If review erases the saving, the pilot failed however fast the model ran.
Decide the kill-or-scale threshold in advance. A widely discussed 2025 report from MIT NANDA, The GenAI Divide, found that 95 percent of enterprise generative AI pilots showed no measurable impact on profit and loss. In its 2025 report, Thomson Reuters found only 20 percent of respondents across professional services say their organization measures the return on AI. If you are not measuring, you cannot know which side of it you are on.
Baseline first, set error tolerances, measure reviewer time. Then capture it in one place: a pilot decision record naming the owner, date, kill-or-scale threshold, and the evidence behind the call. That record, not the applauded demo, tells you whether to scale.
Sources
MIT NANDA, The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025 (via Fortune)
Thomson Reuters, 2025 Generative AI in Professional Services Report, legal executive summary
Related: GenAI transformation for legal teams

============================================================
## Article: AI governance for in-house legal teams: five minimum controls  <https://lexsas.com/insights/ai-governance-five-controls.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
AI governance for in-house legal teams: five minimum controls
The adoption numbers no longer leave room for a wait-and-see posture. In the Association of Corporate Counsel's 2025 survey, US in-house respondents using generative AI rose from 23 to 52 percent in a year. Thomson Reuters' 2025 professional services report found, separately, that across industries 52 percent had no policy on generative AI at work. The same number, two different populations: US in-house adoption and cross-industry policy absence, together a tool outrunning its guardrails.
Before I let a team scale, I ask for five controls on one page.
First, data handling. Write down what may enter a prompt: client identities, personal data and privileged material each need a rule, set before anyone opens a chat window.
Second, accountability. Every AI-assisted output has a named owner. The model drafts; a lawyer signs and answers for it.
Third, human review. Tie review depth to the impact and reversibility of the output, not only to whether it leaves the team. High-impact or hard-to-reverse work passes a qualified reader who can reject it. A later spot check does not meet that bar.
Fourth, logging and records. Log the metadata and review trail: which tool, which version, who approved. Set a retention period and a deletion rule rather than storing full prompts by default. Memory is not a record when a regulator asks how a document was produced.
Fifth, error handling. Agree in advance what happens when the model is wrong: who gets told, how the correction is documented, what changes in the workflow.
These five are the floor for a pilot, not the finished house. The next layer adds a use-case inventory, access controls, and training. None of it needs a new platform or a committee, just one page, the starting document that links out to each control, signed first by the general counsel.
Sources
ACC and Everlaw, Generative AI's Growing Strategic Value for Corporate Law Departments, 2025
Thomson Reuters Institute, 2025 Generative AI in Professional Services Report
Related: Legal tech and AI workflow automation

============================================================
## Article: KVKK-compliant generative AI workflows for legal teams  <https://lexsas.com/insights/kvkk-compliant-genai-workflows.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
KVKK-compliant generative AI workflows for legal teams
Generative AI tools a legal team will touch are commonly hosted abroad. Whether that triggers a cross-border transfer depends on the specifics: where it is hosted, whether staff reach it remotely, and which subprocessors sit behind it. The moment a prompt contains personal data, the team is processing it, and in a typical SaaS setup, transferring it abroad.
The analysis starts before the transfer question. Under Article 5, processing needs a lawful basis. Legitimate interest covers many internal productivity uses but is not automatic; it calls for a documented balancing test, and special categories under Article 6 need their own basis. I treat that balancing document as the anchor of the whole file.
The 2024 amendment to Article 9 rebuilt the transfer regime. Absent an adequacy decision, transfers rest on appropriate safeguards: standard contracts notified to the Authority within five business days, binding corporate rules, or an undertaking approved by the Board, each conditioned on data subjects keeping enforceable rights. KVKK's 2024 activity note records 1,345 standard contract notifications; the 2025 annual report, summarized by GRC Legal, gives 2,497. 2024 was a partial year under the new regime, so I read these as period markers, not a clean growth rate.
The workflow layer is where compliance turns into daily habit. Teams that run this well strip identifiers from prompts where identity adds nothing, prefer enterprise plans that contractually exclude training on customer inputs, and keep records aligned: processing inventory, vendor agreement, balancing test, transfer mechanism, retention terms.
A defensible file is one page per tool: which data goes in, on what lawful basis, through which transfer mechanism. I treat that page as an intake index that links out to the deletion, notice, security and data-subject-rights records behind it. In the phased adoptions that succeed, it existed before the pilot started.
Sources
KVKK, 2024 Yılı Faaliyet Bilgi Notu
GRC Legal, KVKK 2025 Annual Report summary
Related: Legal tech and AI workflow automation

============================================================
## Article: An AI vendor due diligence checklist for legal teams  <https://lexsas.com/insights/ai-vendor-due-diligence.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
An AI vendor due diligence checklist for legal teams
I have sat on both sides of this table. I helped build AI tooling for legal teams inside a vendor, and I have signed off on buying it. The gap between a good demo and a safer contract is a short list of questions; the answers belong in the contract itself: audit rights, breach notice, liability.
The risk is measurable. Verizon's 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report put third-party involvement in breaches at 48 percent, a figure counting all third parties, not AI vendors. IBM's 2025 Cost of a Data Breach report adds that among breached organizations, about one in five said the breach involved shadow AI; heavy shadow-AI users reported costs roughly 670,000 dollars higher than light or non-users, an association, not a proven cause. An unvetted AI tool is a third party with unusually broad access to privileged material.
Six questions have earned their place on my list.
Training data provenance. Where did the model's training data come from, and can the vendor show it had rights to use it?
Prompt retention and reuse. Are your prompts and documents stored, for how long, and used to train anything?
Subprocessors and hosting region. Who else touches the data, how far down the subprocessor chain, in which jurisdiction, and can deletion be verified rather than just promised?
IP indemnity. If an output infringes someone's rights, who carries that?
Audit trail. Can you reconstruct who asked what, and what the system returned?
Exit and data return. When the contract ends, how does your data come back, and in what format?
A vendor with clean answers welcomes these questions. Hesitation on any of them is itself an answer, and it arrives before you have signed anything. Treat it as a signal, not a verdict; the answers still have to land in the contract.
Sources
Verizon 2026 Data Breach Investigations Report
Verizon 2026 DBIR (newsroom summary)
IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2025 (newsroom)
Related: GenAI transformation for legal teams

============================================================
## Article: What generative AI actually changes for in-house legal teams  <https://lexsas.com/insights/genai-for-legal-teams.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 6 min read
What generative AI actually changes for in-house legal teams
When our in-house team built generative AI tooling on AWS Bedrock, the first real decision was not which model to use. It was which single workflow to point it at first, and in what order to add the rest. We started by writing down what the system must never do before what it could do, then picked one narrow, high-volume task to run first. That sequencing choice taught me more than any product demonstration. The useful question is not whether generative AI will automate a legal department away or leave it untouched, but a narrower one: what does it change, what does it leave untouched, and how do you run it so it helps rather than quietly creating risk.
What does not change
Start with what stays put, because it is the part people skip. The duty to reach a defensible answer does not move. A model can draft a position, but a lawyer still owns it, signs off on it, and answers for it if a regulator asks. Privilege, confidentiality and conflicts do not relax because a tool is in the loop; if anything they get harder, since a poorly configured system can move sensitive material to places it should never be. How far privilege even reaches depends on the jurisdiction: in-house communications are not protected everywhere, and in EU competition matters, for instance, they may attract no privilege at all, so a tool that copies such material into new systems only widens the exposure.
Judgment about risk appetite does not change either. Deciding how much regulatory ambiguity a business can carry, and on what terms, is a matter of context, relationships and history that no model holds. The same is true of the harder calls: when to escalate, when to say no, when to say yes with conditions. Those decisions are the actual job, and generative AI does not touch them.
It is worth saying this plainly, because the point gets lost in product demonstrations. A regulator does not accept "the tool produced it" as an answer, and neither does a board. The lawyer is still accountable. That fact should shape every design choice that follows: the question is not whether to use these tools but how to place them so they support an accountable human rather than obscure one.
What actually changes
What does change is the work that surrounds those decisions, and there is a great deal of it. Three areas stand out in practice.
The first is intake. A large share of an in-house team's time goes to routing questions, restating them clearly, and gathering the facts needed to answer. A well-scoped model can triage a request, ask for the missing pieces, and hand the lawyer a clean brief instead of a vague email. That does not decide anything. It removes the friction before the deciding starts.
The second is knowledge retrieval. In-house teams sit on years of prior advice, playbooks and precedent that are hard to search when they are needed. Retrieval over that material, done carefully, means a lawyer can find how the team handled a similar question two years ago in seconds rather than hours. The value is not a generated answer. It is fast access to the team's own reasoning.
The third is first drafts. Standard clauses, routine responses, summaries of long filings and initial cuts of internal memos are all work where a model can produce a serviceable starting point. The lawyer then edits, corrects and takes ownership. When we built generative AI tooling on AWS Bedrock, the first-phase target was a meaningful, measured reduction in manual workload on exactly this kind of task, together with less routine dependence on outside counsel. The point was never to remove the lawyer. It was to move the lawyer's time toward the work that needs a lawyer.
How to run it responsibly
The gap between a useful tool and a liability is almost entirely in how it is run. A few rules have held up.
Set guardrails before capability. Decide what data the system may touch, where outputs may go, and what it must never do, and enforce that in configuration rather than in a policy document nobody reads.
Tie review to risk. High-impact work and anything that leaves the team gets a lawyer's review before it goes out; low-risk internal steps can carry a lighter touch. Treat that review as the operational approval gate, not the whole of responsibility: accountability runs across the design, the configuration and the sign-off, not one moment at the end.
Run measurable pilots. Pick one workflow, define what good looks like before you start, and measure it. A pilot that cannot show whether it saved time or introduced errors has told you nothing.
Track errors honestly. Models are confidently wrong in ways that look plausible. You need a way to catch that, and a team that treats a wrong answer as a finding rather than an embarrassment.
One more point on outside counsel, because it is where the economics are supposed to show up first. Much of what a legal team sends outside is volume rather than hard law: routine research, first-pass review and drafting that goes out because there is no internal capacity, not because the question needs a specialist. Moving a share of that in-house is what teams aim at when they target lower outside-counsel spend. It is a target, not a guarantee; many teams have not yet seen those savings land, while the genuinely difficult matters still go to the firms that should handle them.
None of this requires believing the hype, and none of it requires dismissing the technology. Generative AI does not change what a legal team is for. It changes how much of the surrounding work has to be done by hand, which is worth a lot if you are honest about where the judgment still has to sit. The teams that get value from it stay skeptical about the claims, specific about the workflow, and patient enough to prove each step before trusting it with the next.
Related: Legal tech and AI workflow automation

============================================================
## Article: Where a lawyer's hours actually go  <https://lexsas.com/insights/where-legal-hours-go.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
Where a lawyer's hours actually go
Ask a legal team where its time goes and the honest answer is rarely "judgment". Clio's Legal Trends Report puts the average lawyer's utilisation rate at 38 percent, about three hours of billable work in an eight hour day. Apply the realisation rate and what actually gets invoiced falls to roughly 2.6 hours. Clio measures the billing funnel, not the diary; in my experience the rest goes to intake, chasing documents, status updates and administrative work that no client ever asked to buy.
That is the real case for legal workflow automation. The point is not replacing lawyers. It is recovering the hours that never involved lawyering in the first place. Thomson Reuters' Future of Professionals report projects that AI could free up nearly 240 hours per professional each year, roughly six working weeks. That is a projection of potential freed time, not a measured saving, but the direction is clear.
In practice the gains come from unglamorous places: a triaged intake queue instead of a vague inbox, retrieval over the team's own precedent, first drafts of routine documents. None of this decides the matter. Triage and retrieval feed the decision; the final legal judgment, and the verification behind it, stay with the lawyer. What the tooling does is clear the runway.
The discipline matters more than the tooling. Start with one workflow, measure it honestly, and expand only when the numbers hold.
Sources
Clio, Legal Trends Report (2025)
Thomson Reuters Institute, Future of Professionals Report (2025)
Related: GenAI transformation for legal teams

============================================================
## Article: AI is quietly redrawing the legal industry  <https://lexsas.com/insights/ai-reshaping-legal-industry.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
AI is quietly redrawing the legal industry
Legal work sits near the top of most AI exposure charts. Goldman Sachs estimated in 2023 that around 44 percent of US legal tasks are exposed to AI-driven automation, second only to office and administrative support. Three years on, that estimate reads less like a provocation and more like a project plan.
Adoption has caught up. Thomson Reuters reports generative AI use at 41 percent of law firms and 47 percent of corporate legal departments. Law firm use rose from 28 percent, up by about half; corporate departments moved from 23 percent, roughly doubling. The report frames this as adoption reaching critical mass rather than early experimentation. In a single budget cycle the tools moved from pilot to plumbing.
What changes is not whether lawyers exist but what surrounds them. Research and first drafts can reduce from days to hours. Knowledge that lived in inboxes becomes retrievable. Models that lean on many juniors billing many hours come under pressure. The differentiator shifts from headcount to judgment, and to the quality of the workflows and guardrails around the tools. None of this removes the need for a lawyer; it changes where a lawyer's time is worth spending.
The organisations pulling ahead treat this as an operations question, with pilots, measurement and governance. The ones falling behind treat it as procurement. The technology is the easy part; redesigning the work is the transformation.
Sources
Goldman Sachs, research on generative AI and global economic growth (2023)
Thomson Reuters, 2026 AI in Professional Services Report
Related: E-commerce and platform regulation

============================================================
## Article: The billable hour is running out of logic  <https://lexsas.com/insights/beyond-the-billable-hour.html>
============================================================
Insights
July 2026 - 2 min read
The billable hour is running out of logic
The billable hour survived every earlier wave of legal technology because efficiency and revenue pulled in opposite directions: a faster lawyer earned less. Generative AI breaks that truce. Clio's Legal Trends Report models that around 74 percent of the work US law firms currently bill by the hour could be automated. That is an estimate of what is exposed, heaviest in routine, document-heavy tasks, not a measure of automation already happening. Either way, when the hour itself deflates, pricing by the hour stops making sense.
Clients have already signalled where this goes. In Clio's consumer survey of about a thousand US adults, 71 percent say they would rather pay a flat fee for an entire matter than watch a meter run. That is a stated preference rather than observed behaviour, but it points in one direction. A new generation of AI-native legal businesses, Eudia among the most visible, is building directly against hourly billing, fusing human lawyers with the tools.
What replaces the hour is a menu rather than a single model: flat fees per matter, subscriptions for ongoing counsel, value-based pricing. The common thread is that the client buys an outcome. Who captures the resulting efficiency gain is not automatic: fee rules, jurisdiction and the terms of engagement decide whether it accrues to the firm that built the better workflow or flows back to the client.
For firms the strategic point is blunt. Price the work, and your own automation becomes margin. Price the time, and it becomes your client's discount.
Sources
Clio, Legal Trends Report (2024)
Fortune, on Eudia as an AI-native legal business challenging the billable hour (2025)
Related: GenAI transformation for legal teams

============================================================
## Ana Sayfa (TR)  <https://lexsas.com/tr/>
============================================================
YAPAY ZEKA LEGAL-TECH GİRİŞİMİ
Hukuki işi hızlandıran yapay zeka, güvenceler baştan kurulu
LEXSAS'ı 2026'da, hukuki ve regülatif işi hızlandıran yapay zeka destekli araçlar tasarlamak ve bu konuda danışmanlık vermek için kurdum. Talep karşılama, doküman ve bilgi iş akışları, araştırma ve uyum operasyonları üzerine çalışıyorum; gizlilik, fikri mülkiyet ve yönetişim güvencelerini baştan kuruyorum.
İletişime geçin
Çalışma alanları
20+ yıl şirket içi hukuk
Amazon, eBay, PMI, White & Case
Kanuni yönetim kurulu üyeliği
Ankara Barosu
ÇALIŞMA ALANLARI
Altı alan, tek çalışma standardı
Talep otomasyonundan rekabet savunmasına, her iş güvenceleriyle gelir.
Hukuk teknolojisi ve yapay zeka iş akışı otomasyonu
Talep karşılama, doküman ve bilgi iş akışları, araştırma ve uyum operasyonlarını hızlandıran yapay zeka destekli araçlar tasarlıyorum. Tekrar eden işi sadeleştirip hukukçunun zamanını gerçekten muhakeme isteyen işlere ayırmayı hedefliyorum.
Yapay zeka yönetişimi ve regülasyon uyumu
Gizlilik, fikri mülkiyet ve yönetişim güvencelerini araçların tasarımına baştan yerleştiriyorum. Sorumluluk, insan incelemesi ve belgeleme içeren, denetime dayanıklı çerçeveler kuruyorum.
Hukuk ekipleri için üretken yapay zeka dönüşümü
Hukuk operasyonlarında kur ya da satın al kararını, benimseme yolunu ve ölçülebilir pilotları birlikte tasarlıyorum. Amaç, aracı değil, aracın çevresindeki disiplini kurmak.
Kişisel verilerin korunması ve veri egemenliği
KVKK ve GDPR çerçevesinde sınır ötesi veri aktarımı, taahhütname ve tasarımda mahremiyet konularında danışmanlık veriyorum. Veri egemenliği gereklerini pratik yollara bağlıyorum.
E-ticaret ve platform regülasyonu
Pazaryeri operasyonu, aracı sorumluluğu ve 5651 sayılı Kanun kapsamındaki bildir kaldır süreçleri üzerine çalışıyorum. Tüketici hukuku, reklam ve ödeme temas noktaları da bu alanın parçası.
Rekabet ve dijital pazarlar
Rekabet Kurumu incelemelerinde savunma stratejisi kuruyorum, fiyat paritesi, ürün paketleme ve sıralama uygulamaları gibi konularda çalışıyorum. Algoritmik hesap verebilirlik özel odak noktam.
YAKLAŞIM
Nasıl çalışıyorum
Satıcı değil, uygulayıcı gözü
Bir ürünü pazarlamak için değil, hukuk işinin içinden konuşuyorum. Neyin gerçekten işe yaradığını, hangi kısayolun sonradan risk biriktirdiğini uygulama deneyimiyle ayırt ediyorum.
Hızı mümkün kılan güvenceler
Yaklaşımım evet, şu koşulla mantığına dayanıyor. İyi kurulmuş güvenceler işi yavaşlatmaz, tam tersine güvenle hızlanmayı mümkün kılar.
Ölçülebilir pilotlar, insan incelemesi
Bir çözümün işe yarayıp yaramadığı hislerle değil, ölçülebilir pilotlarla anlaşılır. Aracın hiçbir çıktısı, bir hukukçunun incelemesinden geçmeden dışa çıkmaz.
“Faydalı cevap çoğu zaman düz bir evet ya da hayır değildir; evet, şu koşulla.”
Çalışma ilkesi
DENEYİM
Yirmi yıl, regülatörlerin karşısında
Yirmi yılı aşkın süredir küresel teknoloji ve pazaryeri şirketlerinde şirket içi hukuk, regülasyon, kurumsal ilişkiler ve kamu politikası liderliği yapıyorum; çoğu regüle pazarlarda.
Bu süre boyunca kanuni yönetim kurulu görevleri üstlendim; soruşturmalar, lisans soruları ve pazara giriş süreçlerinde hukuk fonksiyonlarına liderlik ettim. Yapay zeka üzerine söylediklerim bu uygulama zeminine dayanıyor.

Kanuni yönetim kurulu üyeliği
Ankara Barosu
